banner: Week 5 · Prove It — Types of Angles
essential_question: Why do the five angle families never blur into their neighbors at the boundary?
objective: Justify why 90° and 180° each stand alone as exact boundaries and why a reflex angle must exceed 180°, review the week's Math-U-See practice, and pass the mastery checkpoint on classifying and combining angle types.

content.prompt: Without looking, list all five angle types in order from smallest to largest, and write the degree range or exact value for each. Then check against your concept note.

content.heading: Go over Lesson Practice 4A & 4B together
content.note: Bring your completed MUS Lesson Practice 4A/4B. We check the tricky ones: a single set of rays actually names two angles (assume the smaller one unless told otherwise), classifying an angle straight from its drawing, and drawing angles like 190° and 340° that wrap into reflex. Fix any you missed and note WHY.

content.heading: Defend three claims
bullets: Claim 1: 90° can never be called acute or obtuse, no matter how close a nearby angle gets. Argue it from the strict (open) inequalities that define acute and obtuse.
Claim 2: a reflex angle must always measure more than 180°. Argue it from what 'the other side' of a smaller angle has to add up to.
Claim 3: a right angle plus a right angle always equals a straight angle — never anything else. Argue it using plain degree addition, not a picture.

content.preview: Next week, Lesson 5: parallel & perpendicular lines and bisectors — where these angle types show up at every intersection.

success_criteria.text: I can classify any angle as acute, right, obtuse, straight, or reflex from its degree measure without help
I can find the resulting type when two angle measures are added together
I can justify why 90° and 180° each stand alone as exact boundaries and why a reflex angle must exceed 180°

compass.note: Curriculum/Geometry/The Salas Compass/2026-W05 Types of Angles.md

questions.prompt: An angle measuring 90° is classified as…
questions.explain: 90° is the exact boundary between acute and obtuse, so it is its own type: right.

questions.prompt: An angle measuring exactly 180° is a(n) ___ angle.
questions.explain: 180° is two opposite rays forming one unbroken line — exactly the definition of a straight angle.

questions.prompt: Which degree range describes a reflex angle?
questions.explain: Reflex angles are strictly bigger than a straight angle (180°) and strictly less than one full turn (360°).

questions.prompt: A right angle plus a right angle equals a(n) ___ angle. Show the degree addition that proves it.
questions.answer: Straight; 90° + 90° = 180°, and 180° is exactly the definition of a straight angle.
questions.explain: Full credit: shows 90 + 90 = 180 and names straight as the resulting type.

questions.prompt: On the grid, draw and label a reflex angle. Mark the vertex, both rays, and write its approximate degree measure.
questions.answer: Two rays from a labeled vertex with the angle opening more than halfway around (greater than 180° and less than 360°), and a written measure in that range.
questions.explain: Rubric: a vertex with two labeled rays sweeping past a straight line (1 pt) + a written degree measure honestly between 180° and 360° (1 pt).

questions.prompt: In your own words, explain why 90° can never be called 'acute,' even though angles like 89° are very close to it.
questions.answer: Acute is defined as strictly less than 90° — an open range that never includes 90° itself, no matter how close a neighboring measure gets. 90° sits exactly on the boundary, so it belongs to its own category, right, not to acute.
questions.explain: Full credit: names the strict/open inequality that defines acute and states that a boundary value is excluded from it by definition, not by closeness.

questions.prompt: Close: rate yourself on Lesson 4 (Emerging / Proficient / Mastered) and name the one idea you'd want to revisit.
